data.title: Certara appoints Jon Resnick as new CEO; reaffirms FY25 outlook
Onsemi, FORVIA HELLA strengthen collaboration on advanced power tech
data.description:
• onsemi (ON) on Thursday announced the extension of its longstanding strategic engagement with FORVIA HELLA with the adoption of onsemi’s PowerTrench T10 MOSFET technology across its advanced automotive platforms.
  • This new long-term agreement strengthens the collaboration between the companies and positions them to deliver innovative solutions throughout the next decade of automotive transformation.

data.title: Qudian changes name to High Templar Tech
data.description:
• Consumer-oriented technology company in China Qudian (QD) announced rebranding—it will change its name to High Templar Tech Limited and its ticker symbol to HTT.
  • Shareholders passed a special resolution at an extraordinary general meeting on December 10, 2025. 
  • Trading under the new name and ticker HTT begins at the NYSE open on December 22, 2025.

data.title: Stock index futures slide as Oracle sinks post-earnings
data.description:
Stock index futures pointed to a lower open Thursday after Oracle (ORCL) posted mixed results, pressuring the AI trade.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) declined by -0.57%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) fell by -0.73%, and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (INDU) slipped by -0.22%. 

“Bond yields have also moved lower, which partly reflects the Fed and the wider risk-off tone this morning,” stated Deutsche Bank’s Jim Reid. He noted Japan’s robust bond auction and Australia’s unexpected employment contraction as contributing factors.

The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) edged lower to 3.58%, marking a decrease of 2.5 basis points. Meanwhile, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dropped slightly to 4.12%, down by 2.7 basis points. The U.S. 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) fell to 4.74%, slipping 2.1 basis points.

Leading gainers in the premarket included Solstice Advanced Materials (SOLS) +6.86%, Walmart (WMT) +6.31%, and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) +6.08%. Among the notable decliners were Oracle Corporation (ORCL) -11.25%, Jacobs Solutions (J) -5.80%, and Insulet Corporation (PODD) -3.46%.

data.title: China's ZTE may pay over $1 billion to the US over foreign bribery allegations: report
data.description:
Chinese telecoms equipment maker ZTE Corp (ZTCOF) may pay more than $1 billion to the U.S. government to resolve years-old allegations of foreign bribery, Reuters reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

On Thursday, ZTE's Hong Kong-listed shares tumbled more than 12%, while its Shenzhen shares fell by their daily limit of 10%.

ZTE (ZTCOY), which already paid some $2 billion in penalties to U.S. authorities over export violations during President Donald Trump's first term, has for years faced probes by authorities around the world, into alleged bribes to secure telecom contracts.

This year, the Justice Department has moved ahead with a U.S. investigation into ZTE for allegedly violating the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) in South America and other regions, the sources said.

The act prohibits payments or anything of value to foreign officials to obtain business.

U.S. officials are working on a resolution that could see ZTE (ZTCOF) pay more than $1 billion, the sources said, or possibly $2 billion or more, one said, based in part on alleged gains from corrupt contracts.

American suppliers like Qualcomm's (QCOM) Snapdragon chips power ZTE’s high-end phones. ZTE also continues to source components from Intel (INTC), AMD (AMD), and other U.S. firms for its phones, servers, and networking equipment, the report added.

data.title: Immunovant drops 7%, prices $550M stock offering
data.description:
• Clinical-stage immunology company Immunovant (IMVT) priced an underwritten offering of its common stock, with anticipated gross proceeds to Immunovant of ~$550M.
  • Roivant Sciences, the company’s controlling stockholder, has agreed to purchase shares in the offering. All of the shares are to be sold by Immunovant.
  • The company currently expects that its existing cash and cash equivalents, together with the proceeds from the transaction, will be sufficient to fund its operating expenses and capital expenditures through the potential commercial launch of IMVT-1402 in the Graves’ Disease indication.
  • Immunovant offered 26.2 million shares of its common stock in the offering at an offering price of $21.00 per share.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about December 12, 2025.
  • The stock dropped ~7% on Thursday during pre-market hours of trading.

data.title: Basilea partners with Phare Bio to deploy generative AI for antibiotic discovery
Global oil supply drops in November; IEA cuts surplus estimates amid stronger demand
data.description:
The International Energy Agency on Thursday said global oil supply fell by 610 kb/d in November, extending the decline from September’s record of 109 mb/d to 1.5 mb/d.

OPEC+ accounted for over three-quarters of the total decrease, led by sanctions-hit Russia and Venezuela. Russian oil exports declined by 420 kb/d in November.

Global oil supply growth has been cut by 100 kb/d to 3 mb/d for 2025 and by 20 kb/d for 2026 to 2.4 mb/d, to 106.2 mb/d and 108.6 mb/d, respectively.

The projected global oil surplus in 4Q25 has narrowed since last month's report, as the relentless surge in global oil supply came to an abrupt halt. 

World supplies will exceed demand by 3.815 million barrels a day in 2026, which would still mark a record but trims last month’s estimate by 231,000 barrels a day.

Global oil demand, meanwhile, is set to rise by 830 kb/d in 2025 amid an improving macroeconomic and trade outlook. 

"Recent strength in U.S. gas liquids demand has been largely offset by persistent weakness in Europe and accelerated substitution away from oil in power generation in the Middle East."

Nevertheless, refinery outages and impending EU restrictions on imports of products derived from Russian crude have combined to propel product cracks and refining margins to 3-year highs in November, the agency said in a statement. 

WTI crude (CL1:COM) fell 1.22% to $57.75 at the time of writing, and Brent futures (CO1:COM) dropped 1.23% to $61.45.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: ORCL, SNPS, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower on Thursday morning even after the Federal Reserve delivered a widely anticipated 25-basis-point rate cut on Wednesday, its third reduction this year, taking the federal funds target range down to 3.5%–3.75%, lowest level in three years.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Gemini Space Station (GEMI) +13% – Shares spiked after its derivatives arm, Titan, secured a designated contract market license from the CFTC, clearing the way to offer prediction markets to U.S. customers through yes/no event contracts covering topics from sports to economic data. The company said trading will soon be available on its web interface and later on mobile, while Titan also plans to expand into crypto futures, options, and perpetual contracts as part of Gemini’s ambition to build a “one-stop financial super app.” President Cameron Winklevoss said the approval reflects Acting Chair Pham’s pro-innovation stance and the CFTC’s support for emerging markets.
  • Synopsys (SNPS) +3% – Shares rose after the company delivered upbeat FQ4 results, with revenue up 38% Y/Y and adjusted operating income up 36%. For Q1 FY2026, Synopsys guided revenue of $2.37B–$2.42B, surpassing the consensus of $2.36B, and adjusted EPS of $3.52–$3.58, above the $3.46 consensus. It expects FY2026 revenue to have a midpoint of $9.61B, reflecting ~$2.9B of expected Ansys contribution and the impact of recent divestitures compared with the $9.63B consensus. CFO Shelagh Glaser said the company is positioned to set another revenue record in 2026 while integrating Ansys, improving operational efficiency, and capitalizing on expanded opportunities.

Biggest stock losers

  • Oxford Industries (OXM) -24% - Shares tumbled after the company cut its full-year sales and profit outlook despite a solid Q3, citing tariff-related uncertainties as the main headwind. The company now expects FY2025 net sales of $1.47B–$1.49B vs. $1.52B last year and the $1.49B consensus, and it projects a GAAP loss of $1.52 to $1.32 per share due to $61M in noncash Johnny Was impairment charges, compared with earnings of $5.87 per share in 2024. Adjusted EPS is seen at $2.20–$2.40, down from $6.68 in FY2024 and below the $2.90 consensus. CEO Thomas Chubb said early Q4 trends point to a softer holiday season driven by tariff-related product constraints and tougher comps.
  • Oracle (ORCL) -12% – Shares plunged despite the company delivering mixed FQ2 results, with revenue rising 14% Y/Y on the back of 36% growth in cloud and a 66% surge in infrastructure revenue. Principal Financial Officer Doug Kehring noted that GAAP EPS jumped 91% to $2.10 and non-GAAP EPS rose 54% to $2.26, lifted by a $2.7B pre-tax gain from selling its stake in Ampere. Remaining Performance Obligations climbed 15% sequentially to $523B, supported by new commitments from Meta, NVIDIA, and others.

data.title: Automakers flag concerns over EU's proposed local sourcing rules - report
data.description:
Automakers have warned that the European Commission's "Made in Europe" proposals aimed at boosting the region's industrial base could further disrupt supply chains, the Financial Times reported.

The proposals include setting local sourcing targets for parts in products like cars and solar panels. The targets will vary according to the EU's dependency on trading partners, particularly China, and the bloc's ability to scale up production.

Local content levels of up to 70% were initially discussed. But these levels may be reduced amid pushback from several companies, while nine EU member states raised concerns over the possible impact on prices, supply chains and competition. 

A policy announcement was scheduled for this week, but was later delayed. The proposed rules could still change before they are presented on Jan. 28.

Setting complex local sourcing rules would be "very dangerous" because they would be "extremely cumbersome" to calculate, BMW's (BAMXF) CEO Oliver Zipse told FT, warning that Europe would risk being "cut out of the innovation race." 

In a letter to the European Commission, Japanese automakers raised concerns that the proposals would exclude even friendly trading partners.

Katsuhisa Okuda, president of Honda Motor Europe (HMC), said overly restrictive targets could yield "unintended consequences, which could slow down Europe's transition towards clean mobility."

Okuda called for a "made with common values" framework, factoring in "trade liberalization, market economy status and alignment with common political and economic values."

Other automakers want countries such as the UK and Turkey to be included in the proposed sourcing targets, given their large manufacturing hubs.

But auto parts suppliers argued that local sourcing targets were critical to their survival, especially since the EU's higher tariffs on Chinese EVs failed to slow their incursion into Europe.

According to CLEPA (European Association of Automotive Suppliers), targeted local content rules would stabilize investment, prevent further delocalization, and protect highly skilled jobs.

The renewable energy sector also signaled their support for the proposals, including Norway's Nel Hydrogen (NLLSF) and industry group SolarPower Europe.
